item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a phantom screamer vibe and because of that, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy two years! ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been two years since this article. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really hope the dog has its own doghouse folder!
Is a good dog.Deserves it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I like this, too. Although my initial concept about these mp3's are "lost souls trapped into digital music", I'm perfectly fine with them classified as complete cyber anomalies.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I like it! Of course, if my co-author prefers not to have the edit made, I will defer to them.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Neat article. At the end there is mention of the SCPF looking for new ways of interviewing/investigating the entities living in the mp3 files. One potential way of doing that would be to send in a member of MTF Kappa-10 to have a look around. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Further investigation uncovered the presence of several hidden folders in C-2516, titled with variations of the phrase "safe house". Discussion is underway regarding further storage and interaction options for SCP-3131 in an effort to better understand the instances' capacity and motivation for increasingly complex behavior.

To this:

Further investigation uncovered the presence of several hidden folders in C-2516, titled with variations of the phrase "safe house". Discussion is underway regarding further storage and interaction options for SCP-3131 in an effort to better understand the instances' capacity and motivation for increasingly complex behavior. Use of MTF Kappa-10 ("Skynet") operatives as mediators of future interactions is pending review.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Approximately five minutes after the copying process, "dogbark.mp3" was renamed "my.dog".

Well that's just cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
uTorrent is a distribution program, not a file-hosting website. As well, I've only ever heard of the website being referred to as "The Pirate Bay."

Regardless, upvoted because I looove the concept.

EDIT: Actually I just remembered it's actually μTorrent, with a Greek mu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely echoing the "cool setup, but wanes by the end" assessment folks seem to have landed on, the premise here just doesn't feel like it was followed through to its full potential. The interview, while interesting to read, didn't really feel like it elucidated a lot of information about the entity. I did enjoy the bits of characterization though. I think perhaps making the responses a little less oblique would help make it better. I think perhaps some additional development into how the anomaly spreads and whether its one intelligence or multiple which can converge, perhaps through digital file sharing, would be interesting avenues to pursue if you wished to expand on this. For now though, I have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for making me shiver halfway through that interview :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly don't know! \0/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The lyrcis of Tom's diner is really a genius one, in my opinion because it was just about a particular scene in a morning at the diner. As if Suzanne Vega sat at the diner, watched everyone, wrote everything, and decided to make a song out of it, like it was a genuine raw material, preserving a simple scene but with ambiguous meaning. And I love that. :3

Anyway, I'm happy you liked it! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suppose my sticking point then is the interview1 because I'm more 'scared for them' and not so much 'empathizing with them', which gives a kind of mood whiplash when it seems to shift focus.

Like I said with bittermixin, the dread is more like a side effect of the fascination I've felt drafting this piece. Empathy is an interesting term, but fascination may suit the style of the article better, especially at the ending about the safe houses, which I believe you suggested. :)

Which was my favorite part, so I'm reluctant to say it should be edited.

Well, thanks! XD. But like I said, it's because I was trully passionate during writing the interview, as if I was really having conversation to coffee.mp3.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[PLAY MUSIC]
Music playing...

Just the general concept of 'digital sapience talks through song lyrics' produced quite an unsettling image. This Frankenstein's Monster of clips strung together and repeated.

That's the core idea actually. I was really planning on including an additional interview log regarding a supposed "mp3 cannibal", or possibly even a direct interview with this Frankenstein mp3 cannibal thing, eventually makinh a complex conversation through the diversity of music it devoured. That was from the original idea that these mp3s all have the capacity to affect and copy other digital audio to themselves, as if they can see the "audio stream" of each other, the same way they can perceive human speech. I decided to put it beside for now because it may be a very complex thing to do for such inexperienced writer like me.

That still doesn't really make any sense, but in fairness this is only going by a traditional understanding of spirits/ghosts/etc.

One of the stuff I had in mind was that most of the instances where recovered during murders, and the search was only done recently due to the interests of Foundation researchers.

We're led to believe it's going to be another fairly lighthearted, goofy skip, but then we're shot right into another powerful feeling: dread.

Same with Zyn, I wasn't really planning to make the reader feel dread about these mp3s, though I'm sure it'll happen anyway because no matter how much the Foundation researchers seem amused and fascinated about them, seeing their complex behavior, the thought of their origins and the underlying meaning behind their twisted lyrics will put anyone directly to the uncanny valley.

I know 'tragedy' perhaps isn't the intended response here, but this is just assuming you did expand upon this 'mystery woman' idea.

The mystery woman, I'll disclose, is just about how it might've used it's lyrics to evade an interview. It's like, it knows that scaring the researcher may make the interview easier or just make it seem dubious or nonsensical in contextual meaning.

I don't know if this was intended, but doesn't it feel a bit childish? The tone throughout just feels very quick and impatient, as if the Researcher doesn't really care about what the instance has to say.

I think the researcher really wasn't expecting a productive interview. Like what's mentioned from the article, it was the first successful interview, and that doesn't necessarily mean it was meant to be successful. It might imply the other interviews were conducted this short and simple, as they were still experimenting for a better mode of communication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I recently took a class in art/design. For the final project, I created a wordless comic book showing the storyline from "Tom's Diner".
This resonated for me in ways I cannot adequately express verbally. All I can offer is a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is SCP-537 connected to this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good points.

+ show
- hide

no, it was meant to indicate that the explanation was given as a cover story.

Duh-doy, of course it was. Apologies, I misread.

indicate that the anomalous .mp3s were inherently different

That doesn't really need to be reinforced.

the sapience reflects in the memory usage.

That still doesn't really make any sense, but in fairness this is only going by a traditional understanding of spirits/ghosts/etc. It's the Foundation, so I can suspend my disbelief a little.

could you tell us what the haunting bits were?

Just the general concept of 'digital sapience talks through song lyrics' produced quite an unsettling image. This Frankenstein's Monster of clips strung together and repeated.

incidentally, I didn't write the interview or the addenda

That's very interesting. It doesn't really change my stance, but I would have sworn blind on having a lot of input towards the end. It felt very similar to your writing.

Can you suggest ways to spin that material into something that suits the observational log?

As Shaggydredlocks pointed out, there's a loose end in the concept of this 'mystery woman' which I think would benefit from a bit more explanation. It nails the 'don't show your monster' feel, or at least hints at some kind of heart-wrenching past, but falls flat without any proper follow-up.

I think if you'd gone from extreme uneasiness to extreme tragedy1, it would have been more effective. Sort of like the breakneck turn of emotions in SCP-2006. We're led to believe it's going to be another fairly lighthearted, goofy skip, but then we're shot right into another powerful feeling: dread. As it stands, you have this strong feeling of uneasiness followed by a slow dither, and it actually makes the switch more jarring as a result.

I hope that makes sense. For what it's worth, this certainly isn't a bad article, it's just very underwhelming.

Footnotes
1. I know 'tragedy' perhaps isn't the intended response here, but this is just assuming you did expand upon this 'mystery woman' idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suppose I should just ask how I'm supposed to feel by the end, then. If the goal isn't horror, I'd maybe take a look at the interview log, because the portion I quoted seems to suggest that things are taking a dark turn.

They aren't doing those things because they want to do much as a safeguard/proof they deserve to "live" kind of thing.

This plays well with - and flows naturally from the case log. I suppose my sticking point then is the interview1 because I'm more 'scared for them' and not so much 'empathizing with them', which gives a kind of mood whiplash when it seems to shift focus.

Footnotes
1. Which was my favorite part, so I'm reluctant to say it should be edited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it feels like a letdown for there to be this change in tone where they're building a community and reproducing.

They aren't doing those things because they want to so much as a safeguard/proof they deserve to "live" kind of thing.

I thought that was going to be more of a start towards implying this danger

Ohhh. I thought it was fine as-is when you commented on that part in chat. Regardless, we do appreciate your helping out with the article during the editing stage!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• Did you mean purpose? > no, it was meant to indicate that the explanation was given as a cover story. I've trimmed that sentence down so it doesn't use the confusing wording.
  • This is perhaps just a stylistic nitpick, but examples of such websites > added! I've never illegally downloaded music before, so admittedly it didn't cross my mind to put examples in.
  • Ehh, I don't quite understand why or what the purpose of this is. > my thought was that it was to indicate that the anomalous .mp3s were inherently different and the sapience reflects in the memory usage. We did initially think of the "but they were people all along!!!"/"Soylent Green the mp3s is people!" approach, but decided against it in favor of "they know they're not supposed to exist since they're pirated music but they try to exist meaningfully anyway"
  • This felt pretty underwhelming. I'm not sure if Researcher Versova is dim > people in chat liked the interview. Ah well!
  • unless you're implying that the files live in some sort of Toy Story-esque world > no, they're just borrowing from human behaviors. The "new building" naming scheme parallels the "new folder" generic/automatic naming computers use.
  • When you take something initially quite haunting > could you tell us what the haunting bits were?
  • I can definitely feel Zyn's influence towards the end > incidentally, I didn't write the interview or the addenda; I just edited for flow and clinical tone. Can you suggest ways to spin that material into something that suits the observational log (which I wrote about 50% of?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding bittermixin's critisism of the final portion. After the slow buildup, probing at the edges of the horror of this digital sapience1 it feels like a letdown for there to be this change in tone where they're building a community and reproducing.

This section in the interview was a high point for me:

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-3131-275: Before the rain began. There's a woman. Who had died. Cause she sees. Her own reflection. Through the morning. Her hair. Has gotten wet. And so. Does she see me?

Researcher Versoza: Who is this woman?

SCP-3131-275: I'm pretending. Not to notice.

Researcher Versoza: Notice what?

SCP-3131-275: That she's hitching. She is shaking.

…and I wish this was elaborated upon in some way, as it seems to suggest there's something WRONG with their existence, that there is some imperceptible (to us) aggressor or danger lurking somewhere in their universe.

The line you (Zyn) quoted to me in chat (about the safe-houses) was neat, but I thought that was going to be more of a start towards implying this danger - using their behavior as a moment of fridge horror as we become more and more aware that something terrible is happening. As it is incorporated now, it doesn't have that flavor to it. -1, due to building tension only for it to fizzle out.

Footnotes
1. Which was reminiscent of "Season Opener" and that one Sopranos skip, but original enough not to be derivative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spotted some strange word choices and other miscellaneous issues.

+ show
- hide

If possible, the original copies of these instances are to be deleted from the website's server with the explanation of removing infectious malware.

Did you mean purpose? I've never seen 'explanation' used in this context before.

SCP-3131 instances most commonly occur on websites hosting illegal free downloads of copyrighted music.

This is perhaps just a stylistic nitpick, but examples of such websites (Pirate's Bay, UTorrent) would make this feel more like a professional document IMO.

It is noted that while SCP-3131 instances can be read by any suitable media player, doing so requires consumption of nearly ten times the usual amount of memory necessary to play non-anomalous music files.

Ehh, I don't quite understand why or what the purpose of this is. Again, this is heavy nitpicking, but if we're going under the assumption that these are spirits trapped in MP3 files, are we expected to believe that ghosts use up more memory or something? That just seems a bit silly, and feels thrown in to back up the idea of the data corrupting. Because corrupted data is spooky, I guess?

Interview Log 3131-275

This felt pretty underwhelming. I'm not sure if Researcher Versova is dim or just really grasping at straws. For example, here:

SCP-3131-275: The coffee. I am. The coffee.

Researcher Versoza: Your name is "coffee"?

SCP-3131-275: Only halfway.

Researcher Versoza: Did you name yourself "coffee" or you're named "coffee"?

I don't know if this was intended, but doesn't it feel a bit childish? The tone throughout just feels very quick and impatient, as if the Researcher doesn't really care about what the instance has to say. I mean, you've created a personality, I'll commend you on that, but I don't think it's the one you were looking for. Also, as an offhand note, why does the 3131 instance say:

SCP-3131-275: Only halfway.

… which would imply that 'coffee' isn't its full name, but then just accept the name anyway? Doesn't that make this line completely unnecessary? I just feel as though the interview really pales in comparison to the chilling nature of the Observational Log, and that takes the punch right out of the article as a whole, considering they're the two largest blocks of character development we have available as a reader.

Addendum 3131-2:

Cute, but it doesn't really make much sense, unless you're implying that the files live in some sort of Toy Story-esque world in which when the computers are off they're just normal people living in houses and buildings. That's a very heavy thing to imply for what is generally a shorter skip.
No comment on the following Addenda, as it's just a copy-and-paste of my previous criticisms.

I don't know about this one. The quote 'This is the way the world ends. Not with a bang, but a whimper.' comes to mind in that the article had an undeniably strong premise and start, but waned towards the end and ultimately failed to deliver on what it had set up prior. It's almost like two entirely separate articles were spliced together. I can definitely feel Zyn's influence towards the end, and I think that's where the problem lies. Zyn is an excellent writer, but her articles are so great because they are charming and bubbly throughout. When you take something initially quite haunting and then try to make it equally charming and/or bubbly… ehh? It just feels weak.

I was going to give a rather tentative upvote, but upon further consideration I'm just going to hit the downvote. Ultimately, it's a very interesting idea with a good jump-start, but felt bored with itself as soon as the Observation Log ended. Best of luck in your future projects, but this feels like lost potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the safe house idea at the end. Cute. ++1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor illegally-trafficked music file refugees! +1 for giving me reason to view my old music collection as a humanitarian disaster.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
It's been pretty hectic writing this! We spent several hours discussing draft structure and edits in chat. Big, big thanks to everyone who read this over during the editing stage!

This was interesting to write because of the perspective of the .mp3s and how they might see a computer with folders. And how they think of their state of "living" given that these sapient audio files are all pirated music.

Anyway, it is late and I am tired, but thanks again to everyone who read/is reading/will read this!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This idea was initially about Suzanne Vega's1 song Tom's diner, under the curiosity of "what if an mp3 exists that can talk using their lyrics?". So I figured this idea may be a good one and decided to draft a description and interview and posted it on the forums. I wasn't particularly expecting any good feedback since my writing is not in a good shape right now, but somehow Zyn saw interest in the idea.

Anyway, I wrote the draft and Zyn wrote this SCP. To be honest, I'm sad because I didn't write the whole, but at the same, I'm still glad and happy because it's like I made the wormy caterpillar or something, the reviewers (Pedantique, Pendleton_Miller, Shaggydredlocks & Leveritas) the cocoon, and ultimately Zyn made the butterfly. :)

For the draft, click this.
For the thread, click this.

Footnotes
1. The Mother of MP3
